coredata.dc:title: Metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) effect based on silver nanoparticles with different UV spectra on a surface carbon dot-based novel dry platform
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: An, Jia
Hu, Nan
Yin, Chengyue
Liu, Yufei
coredata.dc:description: In this work, to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield of carbon dots (CDs), a novel metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) structure was designed by decorating CDs on silver nanoparticle (AgNPs) film. The glass slide-AgNPs (GS-AgNPs) structure was fabricated using the electrostatic adsorption method, and the AgNPs-CDs structures were prepared by the direct drying method, which then formed the GS-AgNPs-CDs composite structure. In this structure, the MEF effect was found to be size dependent by changing the 5 types of AgNPs with different sizes. And the MEF effect also decreased as the distance between the AgNPs and CDs increased by using polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) to separate the AgNPs and CDs. This hybrid structure can be used as a fluorescence detection platform and the recorded fluorescence intensity of GS-AgNPs 428 nm-CDs achieved a maximum enhancement factor (EF) of 31.72. Considering the high enhancement factor, this system may become promising to find potential applications in biochemical assay fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluorescence analysis
Metal enhanced fluorescence
Glass slide-AgNPs and carbon dots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of AgNPs with different particle sizes
Preparation of GS-AgNPs, GS-AgNPs-CDs and AgNPs-GS-CDs
Investigation of each AgNPs MEF effect

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration of the fabrication process of CDs-AgNPs-GS and CDs-GS-AgNPs. A fluorescence detection schematic diagram and fluorescence images.
(a) UV–vis spectra and distinctive colors of different size AgNPs. (b) Photos of GS-AgNPs and GS-AgNPs-CDs with optimal AgNPs concentrations and CDs concentration.
(a-e) TEM images of AgNPs-391 nm, AgNPs-404 nm, AgNPs-422 nm, AgNPs-428 nm and AgNPs-436 nm.
(a) UV spectrum of CDs. (b) Fluorescence spectrum of CDs. (c) TEM image of CDs and the particle size of CDs was 3.28 ± 0.57 nm.
The fluorescence images of GS-AgNPs-CDs with different types of AgNPs coated with the PVP coating with different thicknesses (gain: ×1).
The EF value histogram of GS-AgNPs-CDs with different types of AgNPs coated with the PVP coating with different thicknesses.

head.ce:title: Metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) effect based on silver nanoparticles with different UV spectra on a surface carbon dot-based novel dry platform

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University), Ministry of Education, Chongqing 400044, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Optoelectronic Technology & Systems (Chongqing University)

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, to enhance the fluorescence quantum yield of carbon dots (CDs), a novel metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) structure was designed by decorating CDs on silver nanoparticle (AgNPs) film. The glass slide-AgNPs (GS-AgNPs) structure was fabricated using the electrostatic adsorption method, and the AgNPs-CDs structures were prepared by the direct drying method, which then formed the GS-AgNPs-CDs composite structure. In this structure, the MEF effect was found to be size dependent by changing the 5 types of AgNPs with different sizes. And the MEF effect also decreased as the distance between the AgNPs and CDs increased by using polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) to separate the AgNPs and CDs. This hybrid structure can be used as a fluorescence detection platform and the recorded fluorescence intensity of GS-AgNPs 428 nm-CDs achieved a maximum enhancement factor (EF) of 31.72. Considering the high enhancement factor, this system may become promising to find potential applications in biochemical assay fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence analysis is a method of qualitative or quantitative analysis of a particular substance using fluorescence spectroscopy. Substances are in an excited state after being irradiated with ultraviolet light, and the de-excitation process of excited state substances can reflect the fluorescence of certain substances [1,2]. As a powerful tool for protein detection, cell visualization, nucleic acid sequence detection and so forth, fluorescence analysis is widely used in biomedical fields due to its high sensitivity and selectivity [1,3]. Currently, various organic fluorescent dyes, such as fluorescent dyes and charge transfer dyes, have been developed to detect various types of markers[4,5]. However, the intrinsic phototoxicity, low signal-to-noise ratio, and easy photobleaching of conventional organic fluorescent dyes limit their applications in biomedical testing. In this context, various semiconductor quantum dots have been introduced as fluorescent sensing materials to address the above issues [6,7]. Although semiconductor quantum dots exhibit strong resistance to photobleaching and chemical degradation, the biotoxicity of common semiconductor quantum dots, i.e. CdSe/ZnS, is still an issue, especially in biological organism imaging [8,9]. Therefore, there is an urgent need to find new fluorescent materials with low toxicity, photobleaching resistance, and no light flashing properties.

Carbon dots (CDs), a new type of quantum dots, have attracted great attention since their first discovery in 2006. Firstly, numerous functional groups make CDs highly soluble in water and allow easy functionalization with various organic polymers [10,11]. Moreover, CDs exhibit unique optical properties, such as highly tunable photoluminescence (PL) from deep ultraviolet to near infrared (NIR) and exceptionally efficient multiphoton upconversion, which can be tailored by controlling the size, shape and heteroatom doping [11,12]. Furthermore, compared to organic fluorescent dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, CDs have better photostability against photobleaching and flashing, better biocompatibility and lower toxicity [13,14]. Therefore, CDs, which have unique optical properties, stable illumination, good biocompatibility and easy functionalization, meet the demand for luminescent materials in current biomedical fluorescence analysis.

However, replacing conventional fluorescent materials is still a challenge due to the lower fluorescence quantum yield of CDs. In recent years, metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) has attracted the attention of researchers [15,16]. When the light is shone on the metal nanostructures, such as gold or silver, free electrons on the surface of the metal are coupled with photons to form localized surface plasmons (LSPs) [17]. When the frequency of the incident light is consistent with the free electron oscillation frequency, the surface electrons will oscillate strongly, resulting in a localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) [18]. On the one hand, LSPR causes selective resonance absorption of incident light by metal nanostructures. On the other hand, it causes a local enhancement of the electromagnetic field around the metal nanostructures [19]. When the fluorescent group is on the surface of the metal nanostructure, the excitation and emission processes of fluorescence can be affected, resulting in enhanced fluorescence. Accordingly, researchers have enhanced the fluorescence of CDs to varying degrees by constructing metal-CDs compounds [20] or using metal island films to excite surface CDs [21]. Jian et al. developed an antibody microarray-based metal-enhanced fluorescence assay to simultaneously profile 7 cardiovascular biomarkers, this work may provide a powerful assist for screening and differential diagnosis of ACS and AAS [22]. Augustine et al. presented a novel approach that integrates a metal-enhanced fluorescence (MEF) biosensor within a capillary flow-driven microfluidic cartridge (CFMC) for the ultrasensitive detection of the Parkinson's disease biomarker, this work demonstrates the successful integration of MEF biosensing with capillary microfluidics for point-of-care applications [23]. Currently, MEF has introduced new ideas for the application of fluorescence analysis in biomedical fields, and there is still room for further exploration by researchers.

In this work, we prepared the AgNPs with different sizes by “seed growth” method and the CDs by wet oxidation method. Based on this, we developed a simple hybrid glass slide-silver nanoparticle-carbon dot (GS-AgNPs-CDs) structure to effectively detect the MEF effect (Fig. 1 ). Through this structure, the sizes of AgNPs and the distance between AgNPs and CDs were varied to investigate the influence of the changing the particle size of the metal group and the distance between the metal group and the fluorophore on the MEF effect.

Sodium borohydride (NaBH4, ≥98.0 %, Sigma), Sodium hydroxide (NaOH, ≥98.0 %, Sigma), toluene (≥99.5 %, Shanghai Lingfeng), Silver nitrate (≥99.0 %, Sigma), 30 % hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, ≥30 %, Shanghai Lingfeng), sulfuric acid (H2SO4, 95 %-98 %, Dongjiang Reagent), nitric acid (HNO3, 95 %-98 %, Dongjiang Reagent), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3, ≥99.7 %, Sigma), trisodium citrate (TSC, ≥99.0 %, Sigma), absolute ethanol (≥99.7 %, SCR), (3-aminopropyl) triethoxysilane (APTES, ≥98.0 %, Sigma), and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP, Sigma) were used.

First, an aqueous solution containing H2SO4 (7.5 mL), HNO3 (22.5 mL) and carbon fiber (0.4 g) was heated to 100 ℃ and stirred vigorously at 700 rpm for 120 min. After the reaction, the aqueous solution was neutralized to pH = 7 with NaHCO3. Then, the aqueous solution was filtered three times, once with a sand core funnel and twice with a 0.22 mm filter membrane. After filtration, the aqueous solution was concentrated using a rotary steamer and dialyzed again with ultra-pure water. After that, the aqueous solution was ultrafiltered using a 3 k ultrafiltration tube, frozen and dried using a lyophiliser. Then, sample (10 mg) was redissolved in ultra-pure water (5 mL) and mixed with NaOH solution (5 mL, 10 M). This aqueous solution was transferred to a high-pressure reactor and heated to 200 ℃ for 24 h. After cooling, the aqueous solution was neutralised by adding HCl. Finally, the aqueous solution was dialyzed for 3 days to obtain the CDs solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of AgNPs with different particle sizes
ce:section.ce:para: AgNPs were synthesized employing sodium borohydride (NaBH4) as the primary reducing agent and trisodium citrate (TSC) as both the secondary reducing and stabilizing agent. The AgNPs are named according to their maximum ultraviolet absorption peak [24]. A brief procedure is as follows:

AgNPs-391 nm: First, an aqueous solution containing 1 % TSC (10 mL) and ultra-pure water (37.5 mL) was heated to 70 ℃ and stirred vigorously for 15 min. Then, 1 % AgNO3 (850 µL) and 0.1 % NaBH4 (1 mL) were added to the aqueous solution, and the stirring was continued for 1 more hour. The ultra-pure water was added to bring the volume to 50 mL after cooling down to room temperature.

AgNPs-404 nm: First, an aqueous solution containing 1 % TSC (1 mL) and ultra-pure water (37.5 mL) was heated to boiling and stirred vigorously for 15 min. Next, AgNPs-391 (5 mL) and 1 % AgNO3 (850 µL) were added and stirred for 1 h. Then, 1 % TSC (1 mL) and 1 % AgNO3 (850 µL) were added and stirred for 1 h. After repeating the previous step, ultra-pure water was added to bring the volume to 50 mL after cooling down to room temperature.

AgNPs-422 nm: First, an aqueous solution containing 1 % TSC (1 mL) and ultra-pure water (40 mL) was heated to 80 ℃ and stirred vigorously for 15 min. Then, AgNPs-404 nm (5 mL) and 1 % AgNO3 (850 µL) were added and stirred for 2 h. The ultra-pure water was added to bring the volume to 50 mL after cooling down to room temperature.

AgNPs-428 nm: First, an aqueous solution containing AgNPs-422 nm (25 mL) and ultra-pure water (20 mL) was heated to 80 ℃ and stirred vigorously for 15 min. Then, 1 % TSC (1 mL) and 1 % AgNO3 (850 µL) were added and stirred for 2 h. The ultra-pure water was added to bring the volume to 50 mL after cooling down to room temperature.

AgNPs-436 nm: First, an aqueous solution containing AgNPs-422 nm (15 mL) and ultra-pure water (30 mL) was heated to 80 ℃ and stirred vigorously for 15 min. Then, 1 % TSC (1 mL) and 1 % AgNO3 (850 µL) were added and stirred for 2 h. The ultra-pure water was added to bring the volume to 50 mL after cooling down to room temperature.
The Ag+ concentrations of the five AgNPs used were AgNPs-391 nm 1 mM, AgNPs-404 nm 3.1 mM, AgNPs-422 nm 1.31 mM, AgNPs-428 nm 1.655 mM, and AgNPs-436 nm 1.393 mM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of GS-AgNPs, GS-AgNPs-CDs and AgNPs-GS-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: In this part of the experiment, we first silanized the glass slide substrate and then sequentially deposited the AgNPs film and CDs on the substrate via electrostatic adsorption and direct drying. A brief procedure is as follows:

Silanization of the glass slide: First, we used ethanol and piranha solution (H2O2:H2SO4 = 3:7 (v/v)) to clean the glass slide. Then, the dried, clean glass slide was placed in 1 % APTES/ toluene solution for 2 h. After that, the glass slide was rinsed with toluene and ultra-pure water sequentially and dried with nitrogen.

GS-AgNPs: First, AgNPs solution (8 µL) was dropped onto the surface of the silanized glass slide substrate and kept in a dry place for 4 h. After drying, the GS-AgNPs were rinsed with ultra-pure water and dried with nitrogen.
GS-AgNPs-CDs: A aliquot of the CDs solution (5 µL) was dropped on the surface of the GS-AgNPs substrate (AgNPs side) and dried for 4 h.

AgNPs-GS-CDs: The CDs PVP solution (5 µL, same CDs concentration as the CDs solution used in the GS-AgNPs-CDs preparation process) was dropped on the surface of the GS-AgNPs substrate (glass slide side) and kept in a dry place for 4 h. The purpose of adding PVP is to make the CDs more uniform after drying.

The SEM images were taken by scanning electron microscopy (SU8020-3 kV, Hitachi). The TEM images were taken by transmission electron microscopy (JEM-1200EX-120 kV, JEOL). The UV spectra were obtained using a UV spectrophotometer (UV-2700, Shimadzu). The fluorescence spectra were tested using a fluorescence spectrophotometer (Cary Eclipse, Agilent Technologies). The fluorescence images were taken using a fluorescent inverted microscope (IX73, Olympus) and a high-resolution microscope camera (Nikon).

AgNPs of different sizes were synthesized using NaBH4 and TSC as stabilizing and reducing agents, respectively. During the synthesis, the small AgNPs grew into large AgNPs in the environment of AgNO3 and TSC. Therefore, this synthesis method was also called “seed growth” method. In this work, the UV absorption spectra of five kinds of prepared AgNPs were characterized by ultraviolet spectrometer, as shown in Fig. 2 a. It is obvious that these AgNPs exhibit a sharp extinction peak at 391, 404, 422, 428 and 436 nm, which shows an obvious redshift state, indicating that the prepared AgNPs are gradually increased. Meanwhile, this conclusion was further verified by TEM characterization. As shown in Fig. 3 , the particle size of AgNPs gradually increased with the redshift of the UV absorption peak and also had good monodispersity and uniform particle size. Through the statistical analysis of the particle size, it can be seen that the average sizes of the prepared AgNPs were 9.82, 30.81, 35.41, 40.20 and 52.07 nm, respectively (Fig. S1). It is worth noting that the half-peak width of the UV spectrum of AgNPs gradually widens with the redshift of the UV absorption peak, which is caused by the poorer uniformity of AgNPs with larger particle size. This conclusion is also supported by the TEM images (Fig. 3). It can be observed that the AgNPs have remained spherical during the process, which may further contribute to the formation of the uniform silver island film under electrostatic adsorption.

The CDs were prepared by the chemical oxidation method described in the experimental section. Their corresponding UV spectral image, fluorescence spectral image and TEM image are diplayed in Fig. 4 , respectively. The absorption spectrum of CDs is mainly concentrated in the ultraviolet region, with strong absorption in the shortwave ultraviolet region, and there is no obvious absorption peak (Fig. 4a). The emission peak of the synthesized CDs is located at 505 nm (Fig. 4b). To match the fluorescence inverted microscope, the excitation wavelength in the fluorescence spectrum was selected to be 400 nm. As shown in the TEM image (Fig. 4c), the CDs exhibit a uniform spherical shape with a size of 3.28 ± 0.57 nm after the ultrafiltration and purification steps. The fluorescence quantum yield (Fig. S2) of the CDs was calculated to be 11.25 % (using fluorescein as a reference).

The glass slide was first silanized by ATPES to form amino groups before the AgNPs solution was dropped onto the centre of the glass slide. The AgNPs were absorbed by the amino groups to form AgNPs films via electrostatic adsorption [25,26]. After the AgNPs were firmly absorbed, the glass slides were rinsed and dried to obtain GS-AgNPs. Then, the CDs solution was added dropwise onto the AgNPs side of the GS-AgNPs, and after drying, GS-AgNPs-CDs were obtained. The same steps with the addition of the CDs (PVP solution) onto the blank side of the GS-AgNPs were used to obtain AgNPs-GS-CDs (the purpose of adding PVP was to make the CDs more uniform after drying). As shown in Fig. 2b, compared with GS-AgNPs samples, there was no significant change compared to the GS-AgNPs samples, but a whiter litter was obscured for the corresponding GS-AgNPs-CDs samples.

Considering the influence of the particle concentration on the absorbance, AgNPs solutions were concentrated or diluted to the corresponding concentration by centrifugation [27]. To obtain GS-AgNPs-CDs with different AgNPs film densities, the concentration of the AgNPs solutions was adjusted while keeping the other preparation conditions unchanged. The fluorescence images and fluorescence intensities of the GS-AgNPs-CDs with different AgNPs film densities were examined, and the corresponding results are shown in Fig. S3-S7. The AgNPs solution concentration corresponding to the highest fluorescence intensity was selected as the optimal AgNPs solution concentration for each type of AgNPs. The optimal AgNPs solution concentrations for each type of AgNPs were obtained by concentrating the original AgNPs-391 nm, AgNPs-404 nm, AgNPs-422 nm, AgNPs-428 nm, and AgNPs-436 nm solutions by 10, 1, 20, 20, and 10-fold, respectively. The SEM images of each GS-AgNPs with the optimal AgNPs solution concentration are shown in Fig. S8. Considering the dual effects of absorbance and AgNPs concentration on the fluorescence intensity of the surface CDs, the AgNPs films with uniform density as shown in Fig. S8 have the best MEF effect under the current experimental conditions. GS-AgNPs with this uniform density can not only ensure a sufficiently strong MEF enhancement effect, but also avoid the AgNPs film being too dense so that the excitation light and the emitted light cannot pass through.

Furthermore, CDs solutions were concentrated or diluted to the corresponding concentration via rotary evaporation. The fluorescence images and fluorescence intensities of GS-AgNPs-CDs (AgNPs-404 nm) with different CDs solution concentrations are shown in Fig. S9. It is demonstrated that the fluorescence intensity reached a maximum as long as the CDs concentration was higher than or equal to the original concentration. Therefore, the original concentration of the CDs solution was selected as the optimal concentration.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of each AgNPs MEF effect
ce:section.ce:para: In this part, each AgNPs MEF effect was investigated using an inverted fluorescence microscope (excitation wavelength: 400 nm; gain: ×1). As shown in Fig. 1, the two optical paths for fluorescence detection were the excitation light generated by the laser device and the emission light generated by the stimulation radiation of the CDs. The excitation light excited the CDs through the glass slide and AgNPs film from the bottom to top, and the emission light entered the camera through the AgNPs film and glass slide from the top to bottom. Considering the influence of the absorbance, AgNPs-GS-CDs with the same optical paths as GS-AgNPs-CDs were selected as a control group for comparison. Additionally, the glass slide (130–160 μm) in the AgNPs-GS-CDs can act as an interval layer to keep the distance between the CDs and the AgNPs much larger than the effective distance of the MEF. Therefore, a more accurate MEF enhancement effect can be obtained in this comparison.

The enhancement effect of MEF was characterized by the average enhancement factor:
(1)
EF =
I
exp
/
A
exp
I
con
/
A
con
Where Iexp and Aexp are the fluorescence intensity and area of the experiment group and Icon and Acon are the fluorescence intensity and area of the control group, respectively.

Moreover, PVP was used as the AgNPs coating to separate the AgNPs and CDs to investigate the influence of distance on the MEF effect. The AgNPs solutions with optimal concentrations were mixed with PVP and incubated for some time to form the PVP coating on the surface of the AgNPs. According to the difference in the amount of PVP, PVP coatings with different thicknesses were formed. The fluorescence images and the EF value histogram of the GS-AgNPs-CDs with different PVP coatings are shown in Fig. 5 and Fig. 6 . We noticed that as the concentration of the PVP solution increased, in other words, as the thickness of the PVP coating increased, the EF values of each type of AgNPs decreased. According to P. Anger et al., we realized that the fluorescent molecules were quenched when they were too close to a metal group [28]. The EFmax values of this experiment were obtained when the PVP solution was 0 mg/mL which means that there was no PVP coating on the surface of the AgNPs. This conclusion may be due to the multilayer CDs structure formed by the high concentration CDs solution. The CDs in the lower layer acted asan interval layer between the AgNPs and the upper CDs while being quenched to ensure that the upper CDs were at the optimum MEF distance. Additionally, it was observed that as the Ag substrate was changed from AgNPs-391 nm to AgNPs-436 nm in the order of the increasing maximum absorption peak, the EFmax values of each AgNPs first increased and then decreased, reaching a maximum at AgNPs-428 nm. These results were attributed to the different characteristic size matching between AgNPs and CDs. Therefore, the synergistic effects of the two factors (spectral matching and distance between AgNPs and CDs) contributed to the decreased/increased EF values, and the optimized fluorescence intensity of GS-AgNPs 428 nm-CDs surprisingly achieved a maximum enhancement factor (EF) of 31.72.

In addition, we utilized the fluorescence lifetime to characterize the fluorescence enhancement mechanism (Fig. S10 and Table. S1). It can be seen that the fluorescence lifetimes of the GS-AgNPs-CDs composites all show different degrees of attenuation relative to a single CDs, and the samples with good fluorescence enhancement have relatively short fluorescence lifetimes. This conclusion indicates that the fluorescence enhancement is due to the fact that the emission wavelength frequency of the fluorophore matches resonance frequency of the AgNPs, as the AgNPs are polarized by the fluorescent molecular dipole at this time, and the coupling between them will increase the radiation rate of the fluorophores to achieve fluorescence enhancement.

In this work, a GS-AgNPs-CDs composite structure was successfully developed as an effective MEF substrate to investigate the MEF effect. The size (maximum UV absorption peak) of AgNPs was varied and the distance between AgNPs and CDs was controlled by the GS-AgNPs-CDs structure. The results showed that as the distance between AgNPs and CDs increased, the enhancement factor (EF) decreased. And as the particle size of AgNPs increases, the EF first increases and then decreases. On the surface of the non-PVP coated AgNPs at 428 nm, CDs can achieve the fluorescence enhancement with a maximum EF of 31.72. This work suggests that the hybrid GS-AgNPs-CDs structure has potential applications in biological testing fields. In addition, the optimized AgNPs with a wider range of sizes can be obtained to determine whether there is a larger EF. Furthermore, this study can provide a universal method to investigate the MEF effect of other metals, and the hybrid structure can also be used as a fluorescence detection platform for cell imaging, ion detection, etc.

Jia An: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Nan Hu: Writing – original draft, Formal analysis, Data curation. Chengyue Yin: Resources. Yufei Liu: Visualization, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work is supported by Chongqing Entrepreneurship and Innovation Support Program (CX201803), China Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (Grant No. 2019CDSQYGD020 and No. 2019CDCGGD304) and NSFC 61927818. Thanks to the analytical and testing center of Chongqing University and Beijing Zhongkebaice Technology Service Co., Ltd for your help in testing work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2024.124744.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Shining carbon dots: Synthesis and biomedical and optoelectronic applications

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Strong two-photon-induced fluorescence from photostable, biocompatible nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots for cellular and deep-tissue imaging
